skill.name: refactoring
skill.description: Systematic refactoring with small-step discipline. Use when user says 'refactor', 'clean up', 'restructure', 'extract', 'rename', 'simplify', or mentions code smells. Enforces one change → test → commit cycle. For structural improvements, NOT style/formatting (use /lint). NOT for adding features or fixing bugs.
skill.summary: Systematic refactoring with small-step discipline. Use when user says 'refactor', 'clean up', 'restr...
skill.author: ArcadeAI

security_audit.summary: The static external command and weak cryptography hits are mostly false positives from Markdown examples and inline code text. No malicious intent or prompt injection was found, but the skill grants all tools and instructs Git rollback commands, which creates operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Overbroad Tool Permission
medium_findings.description: The skill metadata grants all tools with allowed-tools: '*'. This is not malicious by itself, but it allows future workflow steps to use filesystem, shell, or network tools without a narrower permission boundary.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permission is explicit in metadata and has a clear security impact. I found no evidence that it is used for malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Git Rollback Instruction
medium_findings.description: The revert protocol instructs the assistant to run git checkout -- <changed-files> after failed tests. This can discard uncommitted work if applied without checking ownership of changes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is shown directly in a bash block and is operationally destructive. The risk depends on how the assistant applies the instruction in a shared worktree.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution across Markdown examples and inline code. These are documentation examples, not executable skill code, so the command-execution finding is mostly a false positive.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown fences, tables, or inline code references. They are not Ruby backtick execution or executable script content.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptography at the description and key takeaways. No cryptographic algorithm, hash function, encryption routine, or secret handling appears at those lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss refactoring workflow and commit messages only. There is no evidence of cryptographic code or weak crypto usage.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Pattern Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported system reconnaissance at line 160. The line asks whether a refactoring changed behavior and does not collect host, network, process, or environment details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a human review question in the revert workflow. I found no reconnaissance command or data collection instruction there.

dangerous_patterns.title: All Tools Allowed
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares allowed-tools: '*', which removes tool-level narrowing for this workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The metadata value is explicit. The risk is permission breadth, not confirmed malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Git Checkout Rollback
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends git checkout -- <changed-files> as a revert protocol, which can delete uncommitted edits.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is present in the documented workflow. Impact depends on repository state and assistant safeguards.

content.user_title: Refactor Code in Small Tested Steps
content.value_statement: Large refactors often change behavior by accident. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through one tested structural change at a time.
actual_capabilities: Classifies whether a request is refactoring, bug fixing, feature work, or formatting.
Recommends characterization tests before changing poorly covered code.
Prioritizes small refactorings such as rename, extract function, inline variable, and guard clauses.
Requires tests after each refactoring step before continuing.
Defines stop conditions when repeated refactoring attempts fail.
limitations: It does not add new product behavior or fix bugs as part of refactoring.
It depends on available tests or time to add characterization tests.
It can be slow for large codebases because it requires one change at a time.
Its rollback guidance needs care in repositories with uncommitted user work.

use_cases.title: Clean Up Legacy Functions
use_cases.description: Use small extractions and guard clauses to simplify long functions without changing behavior.

use_cases.title: Prepare Code Before Feature Work
use_cases.description: Add characterization tests and reduce local complexity before implementing a larger change.

use_cases.title: Review Technical Debt Tasks
use_cases.description: Turn broad cleanup requests into a controlled sequence of tested refactoring steps.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor One Function
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a narrow cleanup.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the refactoring skill on this function. Keep behavior the same and make one tested improvement at a time.

prompt_templates.title: Add Safety Tests First
prompt_templates.scenario: Code has limited or unclear test coverage.
prompt_templates.prompt: Before refactoring, identify the current behavior that needs characterization tests. Add only the tests needed for the next refactoring step.

prompt_templates.title: Break Down a Large Cleanup
prompt_templates.scenario: A broad refactoring request needs sequencing.
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan this refactor as separate safe steps. Start with the smallest change, run tests, and stop after each green step.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor a Risky Module
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request involving hidden dependencies or brittle tests.
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this module for refactoring risk. Use characterization tests, choose the smallest safe transformation, and define rollback criteria before editing.

output_examples.input: Refactor this long checkout function without changing behavior.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the long function smell, adds or checks characterization tests, extracts one named helper, runs tests, and reports the single completed change.

output_examples.input: Clean up this module and fix the validation bug.
output_examples.output: The assistant separates the requests, explains that bug fixing is not refactoring, and asks whether to switch to a bug-fix workflow first.
The assistant chooses guard clauses as the smallest safe change, verifies behavior with tests, and stops before making additional cleanup changes.

best_practices: Start with tests that capture current behavior before structural edits.
Make one refactoring, run tests, then commit or report that step.
Use the smallest safe transformation before broader restructuring.
anti_patterns: Do not combine cleanup, bug fixes, and new behavior in one refactor.
Do not continue after failing tests without reverting or asking for direction.
Do not refactor untested behavior without adding characterization coverage first.

faq.question: Does this skill change application behavior?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for structural changes that preserve existing behavior.

faq.question: Can it refactor code without tests?
faq.answer: It asks for characterization tests first, then refactors only the covered behavior.

faq.question: Does it handle formatting requests?
faq.answer: No. Formatting and style-only changes should use a lint or formatter workflow.

faq.question: What happens when tests fail?
faq.answer: The skill tells the assistant to stop the refactor, revert the failed step, and try a smaller change.

faq.question: Is this suitable for large refactors?
faq.answer: Yes, if the work can be split into small tested steps with clear stop points.

faq.question: Why is there a marketplace warning?
faq.answer: The skill allows all tools and includes Git rollback guidance, so users should review actions before applying them.
